text: The video shows a robot-assisted central hepatectomy for a hepatocellular carcinoma located centrally in the liver. The patient is positioned supine with a slight anti-Trendelenburg tilt. Four 8mm robotic trocars and two assistant ports are used. The round ligament is divided, and the falciform and anterior coronary ligaments are opened. The dome of the liver is retracted downwards using bipolar forceps. The gallbladder is dissected from the liver bed, and a vessel loop is placed around the hepato-duodenal ligament for potential Pringle maneuver during parenchymal transection.

The surgeon employs the glissonian approach for elective pedicle control, detaching the hilar plate from the quadrate lobe. A small parenchymal incision is made medially above the hilar plate, and the cystic plate is dissected to reveal the right anterior glissonian pedicle. A laparoscopic bulldog clamp is applied for temporary inflow occlusion, and intraoperative Doppler ultrasonography confirms the effectiveness of anterior sector clamping. The intersectoral plane is delineated along the discoloration margin, and the medial plane between the left lobe and segment four is delineated from the round ligament to the middle hepatic vein origin. Transection is performed using a caudal approach following the falciform ligament.

The surgeon dissects and encircles the segment four glissonian pedicle with a vessel loop, then divides it using a haemlock clip. The right anterior sector glissonian pedicle is dissected, controlled, and divided with a vascular stapler. The parenchymal transection along the medial plane is completed, and the origin of the middle hepatic vein is encircled, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon identifies the origin of the right hepatic vein and uses a caudal approach to transect the right intersectorial plane, following the delineated demarcation line on the liver surface. As the dissection proceeds deeper, the trajectory of the right hepatic vein is followed. A cotton tape is used to perform a hanging maneuver, defining the posterior section plane and improving exposure for easier transection.

The specimen is extracted in an endobag through a midline incision. The surgeon inspects the surgical field post-hepatectomy to ensure hemostasis and biliostasis, checking for any bleeding or bile leakage.

The surgeon controls a bile leak from the right anterior glissonian pedicle using interrupted non-resorbable sutures. The surgical field shows the area of the previous hepatectomy, with sutures visibly securing the pedicle.
The abdominal drain was removed on day five after a CT scan and the patient was discharged on day eight.
